children.name: Taeniopygia
children.description: The zebra finches — a small genus of Australasian estrildids known for their distinctively patterned plumage and remarkable adaptability to arid environments. The Zebra Finch is one of the most studied songbirds in laboratory research, particularly in neurobiology, learning, and vocal communication. They are social, gregarious birds that form enormous flocks in suitable habitat.

children.name: Padda
children.description: The Java sparrows — a small genus of stocky, large-billed estrildids native to islands of Southeast Asia. They are notable for their thick, conical bills adapted for husking rice and other grains. The Java Sparrow has been a popular cage bird in Asia for centuries, which has led to extensive introductions across the tropics. They are highly social and often roost in large communal groups.

children.name: Erythrura
children.description: The parrotfinches — a genus of brightly coloured estrildids with green, red, and blue plumage that give them a parrot-like appearance. They are found from Southeast Asia to the Pacific islands, with the highest diversity in New Guinea. Their stout, curved bills are adapted for feeding on grass seeds, figs, and bamboo seeds. Many species are prized in aviculture.

children.name: Lonchura
children.description: The munias and mannikins — a large genus of seed-eating estrildids distributed across Africa, Asia, and Australasia. They are stout, typically brown or black seedeaters with conical bills, often with contrasting white or dark heads and bellies. Highly gregarious, they form noisy flocks in grasslands and rice paddies. Several species have been domesticated and are popular cage birds, including the Society Finch.

children.name: Lonchura domestica
children.description: A domesticated finch derived from the White-rumped Munia through centuries of selection in Japan and China. It is not found in the wild but exists only in captivity, where it is known for its gregarious, non-aggressive temperament and free-breeding habits. Its varied colour morphs include chocolate, pied, and fawn. It is often used as a foster parent for other estrildids in aviculture.

children.name: Lonchura mela
children.description: A small munia with black wings, brown body, and white belly. It is found in grasslands of the Lesser Sunda Islands.

children.name: Lonchura pectoralis
children.description: A munia with a black face and bib, white breast with dark scaling, brown back, and black belly. It is found in dry grasslands of northern Australia.

children.name: Amadina
children.description: The cut-throat and related finches — a small genus of African estrildids with stout cones and distinctive throat patterns. The Cut-throat Finch is named for the male's vivid red throat band that resembles a wound. These are birds of dry savannas and scrublands, often gathering at waterholes in large numbers. They build untidy grass nests in trees or bushes, often reusing old weaver nests.

children.name: Amandava
children.description: The avadavats — a small genus of brilliantly plumaged Asian estrildids with striking red breeding plumage in males. They are among the most colourful small passerines in Asia, with the male Red Avadavat covered in tiny white spots set against a red background. They frequent tall grasslands and wetlands, building domed grass nests with a side entrance.

children.name: Uraeginthus
children.description: The blue waxbills — a small genus of strikingly coloured African estrildids with brilliant blue faces, throats, and underparts. These delicate, fine-billed seedeaters are among the most beautiful African finches, occurring across savanna and woodland habitats. They are active, cheerful birds, often seen in pairs or small flocks at the edges of woodlands and in gardens.

children.name: Estrilda
children.description: The typical waxbills — a large genus of small, delicately proportioned estrildids found across Africa. They are named for the waxy red colouration of their bills, which resembles sealing wax. These active, social finches are predominantly fawn, grey, and brown with fine barring, and many show a distinctive red or black eye-stripe. They feed on grass seeds and small insects, often in large flocks at water sources.

children.name: Estrilda astrild
children.description: The common waxbill, also known as the St Helena waxbill, is a small passerine bird belonging to the estrildid finch family. It is native to sub-Saharan Africa but has been introduced to many other regions of the world and now has an estimated global extent of occurrence of 10,000,000 km2. It is popular and easy to keep in captivity.

children.name: Brunhilda
children.description: A small genus of African waxbills with distinctive black, white, and red facial patterns. They were formerly included in Estrilda but are distinguished by their heavier bills and striking black-and-red facial masks. They inhabit dry savanna and thorn scrub across eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Spermophaga
children.description: The bluebills — a small genus of large, robust estrildids with heavy blue-grey bills adapted for cracking hard seeds. They are found in forests and dense thickets across tropical Africa. Males are strikingly coloured with red, black, and white plumage, while females are duller. They are shy, retiring birds that forage on the forest floor.

children.name: Mandingoa
children.description: A monotypic genus of African estrildid containing the Green Twinspot. It is a beautifully coloured finch of lowland and montane forests, with a glittering spotted breast that gives it its name. Its iridescent green spots are unique among African waxbills.

children.name: Lagonosticta
children.description: The firefinches — a genus of small, beautifully coloured African estrildids named for the brilliant crimson and pink plumage of the males. They are among the most vividly coloured small birds in Africa, with females being duller brownish versions. They inhabit savanna, scrub, and forest edges, often near water, and build domed grass nests with a side entrance.

children.name: Granatina
children.description: A small genus of spectacular African estrildids noted for their brilliant violet, blue, and chestnut plumage. The Violet-eared Waxbill and Purple Grenadier are among the most beautiful of all African finches, with males displaying an extraordinary array of iridescent colours. They inhabit dry savanna and thorn scrub.

children.name: Coccopygia
children.description: A small genus of African waxbills with yellow or greenish underparts and distinctive red rumps. They are among the smallest estrildids, with delicate bills and active, acrobatic feeding habits. They inhabit montane grasslands, forest edges, and cultivated areas in eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Pyrenestes
children.description: The seedcrackers — a small genus of African estrildids with massively thick, powerful bills adapted for cracking the hardest seeds. They exhibit a remarkable polymorphism in bill size, with two distinct morphs occurring within the same population. They inhabit forests and dense thickets.

children.name: Pytilia
children.description: The pytilias — a genus of colourful African estrildids with green or red wings and finely barred underparts. They are active, often confiding birds of savanna and woodland edges. Their flight is fast and direct with a characteristic whirring sound.

children.name: Cryptospiza
children.description: The crimson-wings — a small genus of shy, forest-dwelling African estrildids with red wings, olive-green bodies, and dark faces. They are secretive birds of montane forest undergrowth, often difficult to observe as they forage quietly in deep shade.

children.name: Hypargos
children.description: The twinspots — a small genus of beautifully patterned African estrildids with spotted breasts and bright rumps. They are shy, retiring birds of forest undergrowth and dense thickets, often more easily heard than seen. Their melodious calls are characteristic sounds of African forests.

children.name: Euschistospiza
children.description: A small genus of African estrildids related to the twinspots, with finely spotted breasts and rich maroon or dusky plumage. They are shy, secretive birds of montane and lowland forests, foraging on the ground in dense cover.

children.name: Nigrita
children.description: The negrofinches — a genus of African estrildids with contrasting black, grey, and white plumage, found in forests and woodlands across sub-Saharan Africa. They are active, acrobatic birds that forage in the canopy and understory, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Nesocharis
children.description: The olivebacks — a small genus of African estrildids with olive-green and grey plumage, thick bills, and a distinctive white collar. They are found in forests and montane scrub of West and Central Africa, where they forage quietly in the canopy for seeds and small fruits.

children.name: Parmoptila
children.description: The antpeckers — a small genus of African estrildids with finely spotted plumage and a unique association with ants. They are named for their habit of following ant columns to feed on insects flushed by the ants, a behaviour unusual among estrildids. They have stout, rather finch-like bills.

children.name: Paludipasser
children.description: A monotypic genus of African estrildid containing the Locust Finch, a small, secretive bird of wetlands and moist grasslands. It is named for its preference for grasshopper and locust nymphs, which supplement its seed-based diet. It is one of the most elusive of the African waxbills.

children.name: Paludipasser locustellus
children.description: A tiny finch with brown upperparts, a red face and rump, and pale underparts. It is found in moist grasslands and marshes of Central and East Africa, often near water.

children.name: Ortygospiza
children.description: The quailfinches — a small genus of terrestrial estrildids resembling miniature quail in their shape and ground-dwelling habits. They are found in moist grasslands and marshes across Africa, where they forage on the ground for grass seeds. Their short, rounded wings and strong legs reflect their largely terrestrial lifestyle.

children.name: Amurestes
children.description: A genus of African estrildids with distinctive black, red, and white plumage patterns. The Crimson Seedcracker and related species have massively developed bills for cracking hard seeds.

children.name: Amurestes obscurus
children.description: A finch with a red face, dark brown upperparts, and grey underparts. It is found in forests of Central Africa.

children.name: Sporaeginthus
children.description: A small genus of African estrildids known as the seedeaters or cordon-bleus, with bright blue plumage on the face and underparts. They are among the most beautiful of the small African finches.

children.name: Emblema
children.description: A monotypic genus of Australian estrildid containing the Painted Finch, a strikingly coloured bird of rocky hillsides and arid scrublands. It is named for its brilliant red and black facial pattern that resembles painted markings.

children.name: Stagonopleura
children.description: The firetails — a genus of Australian estrildids with brilliant crimson rumps and tails, found in coastal and forested regions of Australia. They are plump, active finches with distinctive black-and-white patterns on the face and throat.

children.name: Neochmia
children.description: A genus of Australian estrildids with brilliant red markings on the face and rump, found in grasslands and woodlands across Australia. They are lively, active finches that often gather in large flocks.

children.name: Poephila
children.description: The grassfinches — a genus of long-tailed Australian estrildids with black bibs and chestnut or grey plumage. They are birds of arid and semi-arid grasslands, often seen in large flocks drinking at waterholes.

children.name: Heteromunia
children.description: A monotypic genus of Australian estrildid containing the Pictorella Munia, a distinctive finch with a scaly white breast and a blue-grey bill. It is found in dry grasslands of northern Australia.

children.name: Odontospiza
children.description: A monotypic genus of African estrildid containing the Crimson-rumped Waxbill, a small finch with a bright red rump and distinctive facial markings. It is found in dry savannas of East Africa.

children.name: Glaucopectus
children.description: A genus of African estrildids with distinctive blue-grey bills and finely patterned plumage, found in savannas and woodlands of Africa.

children.name: Glaucopectus angolensis
children.description: A small waxbill with a blue-grey bill, brown upperparts, pale underparts, and a red rump. It is found in savannas of southwestern Africa.

children.name: Estrildopas
children.description: A small genus of African estrildids with black, white, and rufous plumage patterns, found in forests of Central Africa.

children.name: Estrildopas ruficeps
children.description: A waxbill with a rufous crown, black face, olive upperparts, and pale underparts. It is found in lowland forests of Central Africa.

children.name: Madagascara
children.description: A genus of estrildids endemic to Madagascar, with olive, yellow, and red plumage. They inhabit forests and scrub across the island.

children.name: Madagascara madagascariensis
children.description: A small finch with brown upperparts, white underparts, and a black face and throat. It is endemic to Madagascar, inhabiting grasslands and forests.

children.name: Madagascara nana
children.description: A tiny finch with brown upperparts, pale underparts, and a short tail. It is endemic to Madagascar, inhabiting grasslands and wetlands.

children.name: Carpophaga
children.description: A genus of Pacific island estrildids with thick bills and brown, black, and white plumage. They are found on islands of the southwest Pacific.

children.name: Carpophaga melanura
children.description: A stocky munia with a black tail, brown upperparts, white underparts, and a thick grey bill. It is found on islands of Fiji and Tonga.

children.name: Carpophaga pectoralis
children.description: A munia with a white breast, brown upperparts, black belly, and thick bill. It is found on islands of Vanuatu and New Caledonia.

children.name: Leucosticte
children.description: A genus of mountain-dwelling finches with grey, brown, and pink plumage, found in high-altitude regions of Asia and North America. They are adapted to the harshest environments on Earth.

children.name: Calliphthalmus
children.description: A genus of African estrildids with bright red or orange bills and colourful plumage, found in savannas and woodlands.

children.name: Calliphthalmus erythrothorax
children.description: A waxbill with a red breast, brown upperparts, and a red bill. It is found in savannas of West Africa.

children.name: Calliphthalmus rubriventris
children.description: A waxbill with a red belly, brown upperparts, and a red bill. It is found in savannas of central Africa.

children.name: Calliphthalmus flaviventris
children.description: A waxbill with a yellow belly, brown upperparts, and a red bill. It is found in savannas of southern Africa.

children.name: Calliphthalmus nigrimentum
children.description: A waxbill with a black chin, brown upperparts, and pale underparts. It is found in savannas of East Africa.

children.name: Entomophaga
children.description: A genus of insectivorous estrildids found in the forests of Central Africa, with olive and yellow plumage and fine bills adapted for catching small insects.

children.name: Entomophaga insectivora
children.description: A small waxbill with olive upperparts, yellow underparts, and a fine black bill. It is found in lowland forests of central Africa.

children.name: Entomophaga chloropoda
children.description: A waxbill with green legs, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts. It is found in montane forests of East Africa.

children.name: Entomophaga flaviventris
children.description: A small finch with bright yellow underparts, olive upperparts, and a thin bill. It is found in forests of central Africa.
